sections.paragraph: m. and once יַנְשֹׁוף ( Isa 34:11), an unclean bird, probably aquatic or living in marshes; Levit. 11:17 Deu 14:16, an inhabitant of deserts or marshes, Isa. loc. cit. LXX. and Vulgate render it ibis, i.e. the Egyptian heron. Chald., Syr., noctua, ulula; and so Bochart (Hieroz. P. II, p. 281, seq.), who supposes it to be so called from twilight (נֶשֶׁף). It seems to me to be a kind of heron or crane, such a one as utters a sound like the blowing of a horn, like the ardea stellaris (Rohrdommel), ardea Agami (der Trompetervogel), grus vulgaris; this opinion being in accordance with the etymology from נָשַׁף to blow. In the list of unclean birds, Levit. loc. cit. this bird is followed by תִּנְשֶׁמֶת, which is almost from the same root (נָשַׁם = נָשַׁף).
